title: How to Parent with Purpose: Building Meaning and Resilience in Your Children
guestBio: Pam Roy is a writer, speaker, and visionary thought leader who reimagines parenting, education, and purpose in our rapidly changing world. She is the author of Parenting from the Passenger Seat and Viktor E. Frankl: The Search for Meaning, a partner at the Viktor Frankl Institute of America, and an executive producer of films spotlighting the youth mental health crisis.
excerpt: What if the key to raising resilient children isn't about controlling their path, but guiding them to find their own meaning? Pam Roy shares how Viktor Frankl's timeless wisdom can transform your parenting approach and help your kids thrive in an uncertain world.
metaDescription: Learn how to parent with purpose and raise resilient children through meaning-centered parenting. Expert insights from Pam Roy on The Manage Her podcast.
keyTakeaways: Parenting from the passenger seat means guiding your children without controlling their journey, allowing them to develop their own sense of purpose and direction.
Meaning is more essential than happiness for building resilience and mental health in children.
Discomfort and challenges are necessary for building resilience—protecting children from all difficulties actually weakens them.
True belonging comes from authentic connection and shared values, not from fitting in or conforming to others' expectations.
Teaching children to find meaning in their struggles helps them develop the mental tools to handle life's inevitable challenges.
Community-based learning and mentorship provide more valuable real-world experience than traditional classroom settings alone.
Traditions and rituals create powerful anchors of meaning and connection that help combat the loneliness epidemic affecting young people.

pullQuotes.text: Raising resilient, purpose-driven kids begins with our own courage to live authentically.
Meaning is essential for parenting, education, and mental health—more than happiness alone.
When we parent from the passenger seat, we guide without controlling, allowing our children to find their own path to purpose.

timestamps.label: Pam's background and journey to meaning-centered parenting
Viktor Frankl's influence on modern parenting
Parenting from the passenger seat philosophy
The B-Unbound model for community learning
Traditions and belonging in healing loneliness

content:
As mothers and leaders, we want nothing more than to see our children thrive. But what if everything we've been taught about successful parenting is missing the most crucial element? What if achievement, happiness, and protection from struggle aren't the keys to raising resilient kids?

In this powerful conversation, Aimee sits down with Pam Roy, a thought leader who's reimagining what it means to parent with true purpose. Pam's work, deeply influenced by Holocaust survivor and psychiatrist Viktor Frankl, offers a revolutionary approach to raising children who can not just survive but find deep meaning in whatever life throws their way.

The Power of Meaning Over Happiness

Pam's journey into meaning-centered parenting began with a profound realization: our culture's obsession with keeping children happy is actually failing them. Meaning, not happiness, is what builds the psychological resilience our kids desperately need. Viktor Frankl, who survived Nazi concentration camps and went on to develop logotherapy, understood that humans can endure almost anything if they can find meaning in their suffering.

This insight transforms how we approach the inevitable challenges our children face. Instead of rushing to fix every problem or eliminate every discomfort, we can guide our children to discover what these experiences might teach them and how they might contribute to their growth and purpose.

As Pam explains, when we focus solely on our children's immediate happiness, we rob them of the opportunity to develop the mental muscles they'll need for life's bigger challenges. This doesn't mean we want our children to suffer, but rather that we recognize struggle as an inevitable and valuable part of the human experience.

Parenting from the Passenger Seat

One of Pam's most compelling concepts is learning to parent from the passenger seat. This approach requires tremendous courage from us as mothers because it means stepping back from the driver's seat of our children's lives and trusting them to navigate their own path while we provide guidance and support.

Parenting from the passenger seat means we're present and engaged, but we're not controlling the destination or micromanaging every turn. We're offering wisdom, asking powerful questions, and helping our children develop their own internal compass rather than constantly telling them which way to go.

This shift can feel terrifying, especially for high-achieving women who are used to managing and optimizing everything in their path. But Pam's research and experience show that children who are allowed to struggle, make mistakes, and find their own meaning become far more resilient than those who are constantly directed and protected.

The Necessity of Discomfort

Perhaps one of the most challenging aspects of Pam's philosophy for us as loving mothers is embracing the idea that our children need discomfort to grow. When we constantly smooth the path ahead of our kids, we're actually weakening their ability to handle adversity.

This doesn't mean throwing our children into dangerous or traumatic situations, but rather allowing them to experience age-appropriate challenges and supporting them as they learn to work through difficulties. Whether it's letting a toddler struggle with a difficult puzzle, allowing a teenager to experience the natural consequences of poor planning, or supporting a young adult through a career setback, these moments of discomfort become opportunities for growth and meaning-making.

Pam's work shows that children who learn to find meaning in their struggles develop what psychologists call post-traumatic growth. They don't just bounce back from adversity; they become stronger and more purposeful because of it.

Building Community and Belonging

The conversation also explores Pam's innovative B-Unbound model, which recognizes that traditional education often fails to prepare young people for real-world challenges and meaningful work. This community-based learning approach connects young people with mentors and real-world experiences that help them discover their purpose and develop practical skills.

True belonging, as Pam explains, comes not from fitting in but from authentic connection around shared values and purpose. When we help our children find communities where they can contribute meaningfully and be known for who they truly are, we're giving them one of the greatest gifts possible in our increasingly isolated world.

The loneliness epidemic affecting young people today isn't solved by more social media connections or activities, but by deeper relationships built around shared meaning and purpose. As mothers, we can facilitate these connections by being intentional about the communities we expose our children to and the traditions we create in our own families.

Living Authentically as Mothers

Perhaps the most profound insight from this conversation is that raising purposeful, resilient children begins with our own willingness to live authentically and embrace meaning in our own lives. Our children are watching how we handle adversity, how we find purpose in our struggles, and how we contribute meaningfully to the world around us.

This means we must be willing to examine our own relationship with discomfort, meaning, and purpose. Are we modeling the kind of resilience and authenticity we want to see in our children? Are we finding meaning in our own challenges rather than just trying to eliminate them?

As Pam reminds us, we cannot give our children something we don't possess ourselves. If we want to raise children who can find meaning and purpose in any circumstance, we must be willing to do that work in our own lives first.

The path forward isn't about perfect parenting or eliminating all struggles from our children's lives. It's about courageously guiding them toward their own sense of meaning and purpose while modeling resilience and authenticity in our own journey. This is how we raise the next generation of leaders who won't just achieve success but will find deep meaning and contribute powerfully to the world around them.

guestLinks.label: 📕 Learn more about the Viktor Frankl Institute

quiz.title: What's Your Parenting Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally guide the young people in your life and what that reveals about your leadership approach. Whether you're a parent, mentor, or someone who influences the next generation, this quiz will help you understand your unique style.

questions.question: When someone you care about is facing a difficult challenge, your first instinct is to:

options.text: Ask them what they think they should do and help them explore their options
Offer to help solve the problem or remove obstacles from their path
Help them break down the challenge into manageable steps with clear actions
Connect them with others who have faced similar challenges and remind them they're not alone

questions.question: When you think about what makes life fulfilling, you believe it's most important to:

options.text: Find purpose and meaning, even when things are difficult
Feel safe, supported, and free from unnecessary stress
Have clear goals and the resources needed to achieve them
Feel deeply connected to people who truly understand you

questions.question: If someone you're mentoring makes a mistake that will have natural consequences, you're most likely to:

options.text: Let the consequences happen while being available to process the experience with them
Try to minimize the consequences or help them avoid the worst outcomes
Use it as a learning opportunity to create better systems for next time
Make sure they know mistakes don't change how you feel about them and that everyone struggles sometimes

questions.question: Your approach to building confidence in others focuses on:

options.text: Helping them recognize their own strength by reflecting on challenges they've already overcome
Making sure they feel supported and know you believe in their abilities
Setting up opportunities for small wins that build toward bigger successes
Creating environments where they feel accepted and can develop authentic self-expression

questions.question: When someone you lead is feeling overwhelmed or discouraged, you tend to:

options.text: Help them find meaning or purpose in what they're experiencing
Look for ways to reduce their stress or take some burden off their shoulders
Help them organize their priorities and create a clearer path forward
Remind them of their strengths and surround them with encouraging community

questions.question: In your ideal vision, the people you influence would grow up or develop to be:

options.text: Self-directed individuals who can find meaning and strength even in difficult circumstances
Confident people who know they're loved and supported no matter what happens
Capable individuals with strong skills and clear direction for their lives
Authentic people who maintain deep connections and contribute to their communities

results.title: The Empowering Guide
results.description: You naturally understand that real growth happens when people navigate their own challenges with support, not rescue. You have an intuitive sense that meaning trumps momentary comfort, and you're willing to let others struggle in service of their long-term development. Your leadership style creates resilient, self-directed people who trust their own judgment. This episode will give you the framework and confidence to lean even deeper into this powerful approach.

results.title: The Protective Advocate
results.description: Your instinct to shield and support comes from a place of deep love and care. You see potential everywhere and want to clear the path for others to succeed. While this compassion is one of your greatest strengths, you might sometimes wonder if you're doing too much for the people you lead. This episode will help you discover how to channel your protective energy in ways that actually build strength and resilience in others.

results.title: The Strategic Planner
results.description: You believe in preparation, structure, and setting people up for success through careful planning and clear expectations. You naturally think several steps ahead and want to ensure others have every advantage. Your methodical approach creates stability and confidence in those you lead. This episode will show you how to balance your excellent planning instincts with space for others to find their own meaning and purpose in unexpected moments.

results.title: The Connected Collaborator
results.description: You understand that belonging and community are essential for growth, and you naturally create environments where people feel seen and valued. You know that authentic connection fuels motivation better than external pressure ever could. Your ability to build genuine relationships is a rare leadership gift. This episode will deepen your understanding of how to use community and shared meaning to build even more resilience in the people you guide.

transcript:
**The Manage Her® Podcast**
*Hosted by Aimee Rickabus*
*Guest: Pam Roy*

**Aimee:** Today's guest is someone who speaks right to the heart of the mission. Pam Roy is a writer, speaker, and visionary thought leader reimagining education, parenting, and purpose in the modern world. She's the author of Parenting from the Passenger Seat and Victor E. Frankl, The Search for Meaning: 21 Reflections on Living Life with Purpose, both deeply reflective guides to helping our children and ourselves live more connected and purposeful lives. 

Pam is also a partner at the Victor Frankl Institute of America, a champion of the Unbound learning model and involved in film productions that address the mental health crisis. Whether she's writing, speaking, or creating new frameworks for the future of learning, her work is always grounded in one essential question: What gives life meaning? Today we're diving into identity, legacy, and how women, especially mothers, can help the next generation thrive with purpose. Welcome, my friend Pam. It's so nice to have you on the show. Thank you for coming on.

**Pam:** I am so excited to be here and I'm excited to talk with you about all these wonderful subjects, what we're seeing in the world because I read your book and I know that there's a lot of things that we have in common regarding our missions, our own personal experiences and awakening to our responsibility to do something about that. So I am equally thrilled to be here with you.

**Aimee:** Thank you so much. Well, let's start with something close to your heart, which is meaning. Why is meaning such a crucial foundation, especially in parenting and education?

**Pam:** I really didn't understand the concept of meaning for probably the first part of my life. I'm 65. I love being 65. I love getting older. Just to make a note of that for all of the ladies out there who might be worried about that - it really gets better and better and better.

But really once I read Victor Frankl and I understood what meaning, how he defined meaning as the primary motivation for living and also a key factor in mental health and wellness, it just resonated so strongly. It became a central theme of everything I do and what I want to put out in the world. And he really looked at meaning not as oh how does this make me feel good. It was about what am I, my unique being in this specific situation, supposed to contribute and we know it. We have this inner knowing. We just don't always listen to it. 

So that became my mission is to talk about the ways we can find meaning and how important meaning is to our lives. I mean it's like oxygen. When we don't have it, we know it. And this is a time where unfortunately in our mechanistic society, meaning is not always front and center.

**Aimee:** No, it's so true. It really is. Once you have it, you know you have it. And when you don't have it, you definitely know you don't have it. But it's kind of one of those things that needs to be more intentional. People need to be intentional about seeking meaning in their life, right? So, I love that. I think that's very important.

And so, you've written about parenting from the passenger seat. Could you unpack that idea for our listeners, especially the moms who feel the pressure to control the wheel?

**Pam:** Yes. And it's a natural normal feeling in this control world to ensure that our children are safe and secure for the future. The challenge is that's impossible. And the future is unknown and unknowable and we are all co-creating it as we move moment to moment. So our need to control and help our children is actually detrimental to them listening to themselves and helping themselves. 

Again we have them for maybe 21 years, maybe 18, whatever it is or longer in today's age. And it's about really preparing them for the transitions in life because that is where we are. We're in constant transition whether it's in work, whether it's personal - pandemics happen all the time. Guilt, suffering, and death.

So Victor Frankl when he did his medical practice and developed his theory, he was working with suicidal patients. So he was a psychiatrist from the early 1900s born in 1905 in Vienna, Austria and he became a correspondent with Sigmund Freud. So psychology was just being born and he became the student of Alfred Adler, another prominent psychologist. And one was saying that pleasure is our primary motivation. Another was saying no, we want power. And he's saying no, there's something more. We are meant - there is something for us to do and we need to listen to that and respond to it. Life is asking us.

I wanted to write a book about my own journey through the parenting process. I have three daughters who are now 24, 26, and 28 out in the world. And I grew up at a time when it was very much - it didn't matter what other kids in high school were doing. Some would go to work, some would go to college. It wasn't college - everybody has to go down this single trajectory.

But my dad was a first generation American. So was my mom. And he was very much no, you got to go in the business world and do all these things. And I was like, "Okay, that's what I'll do." Got good grades, got a business major, went out into the workforce, and became a partner when I was 27, was very successful, young, and then at about 30, my stomach wasn't feeling so good. I'm thinking, what is going on? 

I always wanted to be a mom, but I was following the achievements and the accolades. I had the car, I had the condo, I had the management position. Check, check, check. And yet what I was truly meant for didn't happen until I got married, stepped off that trajectory, which I actually quit my job without another job. I mean, I took three months. I gave my partner notice to say, "Look, I don't know what I'm doing, but I can't do this anymore." And I just kind of trusted life, which ever since I've learned to jump off that cliff, trust it's going to show me the way. And every time it always has.

So, I had these experiences prior to raising my daughters, and the 40-year gap between my education and theirs. Whoa. It just highlighted this mechanistic, put your kid on a conveyor belt, we'll handle it, we'll get them a good job, and as soon as they get a good job and have money, they're going to be set for life. And I knew that that is not what life is about. It's about the ongoing relational connections.

We've developed a society of systems which I talk about in the book. We've got the education system, the health care system or the sick care system, depending on how you want to look at it, the criminal justice system - we have these systems that we feed people into so we don't have to deal with it in our communities. And so these are transactional. We now have become a means to an end. We're a number when we walk in those doors. We are not a unique individual with responsibility for the lives we've been given and listening to our conscience about ways that we can contribute.

And I just really wanted to write a book about how to incorporate meaning and purpose into lives and that there's more than academic achievement or checking the boxes. And this is similar - your book is fantastic with telling the same story so I feel like I'm telling you the story that you already wrote about - this same thing that you discovered. And in many ways we've lost ourselves to this mechanistic world that demands so much of us, so much of us as the nurturers of society - women and our very intuitive, nurturing sense.

Gabor Maté in his book The Myth of Normal wrote about 75,000 women with breast cancer that he studied and the single common denominator he found was they gave up too much of themselves. Where do we nurture in our breasts? And so I was very concerned with how much we're being called to feed this mechanistic world that's very transactional when we humans are relational. It is not how we live. We are meant to engage with the world. We're meant to engage with each other.

We can listen to our conscience to guide us. We can trust it and we can act on it. Listen, trust, act, repeat. That's what meaning is. It's following the pull moment to moment of what we intuitively understand. But when we're so busy checking boxes of what others are telling us we're supposed to do, we're trying to conform so that we don't look so different.

I put my kids in alternative education when they were young. Yeah, so I was that mom that felt so strongly that everyone looked at saying, "Who's she?" And greatest decision of my life. My kids would say, "How come we couldn't do all this soccer?" I'm sorry. You could raise your kids how you want, but I felt so strongly that their relationships with each other, within our family, with the grandparents, with volunteering in the community, with having downtime to just sleep and rest and play was critical. And I really did my best to protect that imperfectly.

I will just say I was constantly getting caught in what am I doing and having those moments as you point out in your book where you suddenly realize whoa this is me - this moment where I suddenly had to look at myself and say how am I contributing to this and do I really have control over another human being's life?

Victor Frankl talked a lot about the multi-dimensionality of humans, we're not just a mind and a body. We can't be programmed or trained. We have a spiritual dimension. And he distinguished this - he called it the noetic dimension to distinguish it from anything religious - that even though there's a greater creator and ultimate meaning and all of that, it's really our spirit, our guide that we know. It's our conscience that's tapping into something we don't even understand. But it plays a powerful role and when we don't listen to it, it can manifest in physical and emotional issues.

So there I was with the girls and Parenting from the Passenger Seat popped into my head one day because we can never drive another person's life, another human spirit. We can't. We don't have that power. And even if it's your mother or your brother, your spouse or your sister, doesn't matter. Your children - we are each our own being. So, we can only ride alongside and do our best to guide and to love.

But, as I'm sure you know with your children, and as I certainly know with mine, and especially now that they're out in the world, they do things and you're thinking, "Maybe that's not so good." "No, I'm doing it, Mom." Okay. So I had to look at myself and say, what am I doing? Because the only thing I can really do is model what it means to live a meaningful life and to express myself out in the world in a way that is authentic to myself and hope that that is something that inspires my children to do the same and other children as well.

**Aimee:** I love that. Yeah, it's so important. It's all you can do, honestly. In my book, I really examine radical ownership of yourself. And I think everybody really needs to radically own their participation in things, but you can never really control what another person around you is going to do. You just have to - you can have your own limits and say, this is a limit or a boundary for me. 

But it's very interesting in the world and life and becoming and parenting and marriage and all of those things. I think one of the lessons that has come back again and again to me is like well you can own you and you're allowed to own you.

**Pam:** Yes. Absolutely. When I was reading your book I had an epiphany about an area of my life even today because they all pop up right - you think oh I've gone around, I can move past that - suddenly it comes around in a different form. But I had an epiphany of a business relationship where I felt that my boundaries were not as strong as they should have been. And I was reading that and I was thinking oh wow yes I have let that go and so I agree totally - intuitively I know it but I just didn't -

**Aimee:** It's so funny how we can feel something in our body and even though we know it's wrong or it's not right or we're just not really in alignment with ourselves on something. Sometimes we allow things to happen. And being able to own those things in our life when something isn't serving us when you're in a business relationship or you need to set a boundary with someone and really be clear on it - you're allowed to do that. I think that's one of the most beautiful things that happened to me in my 40s was I can't control anyone else but I am allowed to control me.

**Pam:** Yes. And I think that that's that evolutionary part of maturing that is really - if we want to embrace it, it's something that I think empowers us. The first part of our life, we're answering all of our conditioning demands of what we're supposed to be and what society has taught us and our families. And suddenly one day we wake up and we say, "Wait a minute, there's something more. There's something different and I'm not paying attention to it and I got to begin with me because the only change" - as Gandhi said is be the change you want to see in the world. We have to start with ourselves.

**Aimee:** Yeah, absolutely. That radical ownership. I was like, "Oh, okay. This is cool. I need to write a book about this." It's good. And I think it's so important that we express ourselves no matter what. I mean, I'm not doing anything that was a dream of mine to do. Not one thing. My life 10 years ago was very different. I just began following that bouncing ball and responding to it. And it's led me to incredible places that I never ever expected. So, I think it's learning to put ourselves out there, which can be very scary, as I'm sure you've experienced, too.

**Pam:** Yes. So scary, right? The scariest thing I've ever done in my life. I really remember the day of my book launch. I thought I really could just crawl under a rock and die. I'm so scared right now.

**Aimee:** Yes. Me, too. I mean, I have that same thing. When I started my blog and I started writing about all these things I was seeing and my experiences - I had gone back to get a master's degree in school counseling when I was noticing a lot of things happening in my community and just so much mental distress. And then going to a very elite college to get a master's degree, I was thinking, "Oh, wow. They don't have it. They're not preparing. They're a commodity and we're the consumers and they're pushing us through" and they were so disconnected from what was happening in the world and it was eye opening. 

So I began writing a blog just talking about it and that's what got the ball rolling to all the other things that I've ended up doing - each one very scary but I kind of close my eyes and jump and think why am I here? As Mary Oliver says, "What do you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?" You got to go for it. And but also my children because I want to model for them what it means to be alive and to fully go for life.

**Pam:** Same for me. Same for me. It was like, "Okay, I'm not doing this for me. I'm doing this for my daughters, for the next generation. Just put this out there. Don't be afraid. It's okay to have a legacy because this life is short," and people will say there will always be people that don't agree with you and that's okay. You're allowed to not agree with my ideas, but it may resonate with one person. If it can help one person, it's worth doing.

**Aimee:** I always say that. And my book, Parenting from the Passenger Seat, is not trying to convince anybody to do anything different. It honors whatever your choices are, but it was really written to validate these women and these parents that have this intuitive sense that something's not right. It's kind of like when I read Victor Frankl's work and suddenly he gave me words and he gave me a framework for what I felt and I thought, "Ah, yes, this is it." 

And so I had hoped to do that and really not focusing on outcomes at all - put the book out there. I mean, I haven't really marketed it a ton. Just life has taken over, but it's something I thought, well, at least get it out there and the rest will take care of itself. But my job was to express myself and put it into the world. What the world does with it, I don't know. Not my problem.

**Pam:** Yeah, totally. No, you're right. I feel like these books kind of come through you and it's like you birth them as authors. I don't think the words came from me. I think they came through me when I write.

**Aimee:** Yes. Absolutely. Absolutely. And suddenly they'll just come fast. I'll be walking the beach or going to the grocery store and I'm pulling out receipts, writing notes on them, or I dictate. I know you dictate.

**Pam:** I do. I do. I talk into my phone. I'll just - my kids will know, "Oh, mom, are you writing a book?" I'm like, "Oh, yeah. Hold on. I'm just talking it into my phone right now."

**Aimee:** Yes. So, tell me a little bit. So, you work closely with the Victor Frankl Institute of America and I think you're a co-founder on that one, too.

**Pam:** Yes, I was a co-founder on it.

**Aimee:** Yes. So, tell me how has Frankl's philosophy shaped your personal and professional life?

**Pam:** Oh, it's transformed my life. I think it's given me courage. Tremendous courage. When I started my blog, I had a friend - I think that there's synchronicities in life when you align with your authentic self. Suddenly the people and the situations come in that support the direction you're moving in. So a friend of mine knew Victor Frankl's grandson, Alex Vesely Frankl, and he was living in California at the time, even though he's from Vienna. He lives there now. 

And she had told him about my blog. He read it. He loved it. We had a three-hour lunch. We're talking about all the views about education that Victor Frankl had a hundred years ago - that you can't standardize human beings and push them through a machine even in the workforce. And so anyway, so we became friends.

I became an executive producer on the Man's Search for Meaning feature film and I thought, wow, we need to provide some background of his work that isn't a detailed book. He could be a dense writer. His subject matter is written for a different time and could be very difficult for people to read. And so, my best friend Mora - 45 years we've been best friends - she's like, Pam, Frankl resonated with her, too. She said, "I'd love to write it with you." And so, she and I wrote that and then we thought, well, let's start an institute.

Right now, it's a resource center of information about Frankl that's accurate to the family because there's a lot of stuff out there that is not correct - like that he went to the concentration camp, came out with the theory. He'd been a doctor, medical doctor, psychiatrist and neurologist for many many years and he worked with suicidal patients. So he had already developed his theory called logotherapy, healing through meaning, and then he lived it in the concentration camps. He saw it was an unwanted laboratory.

So the three of us decided we would launch the Victor Frankl Institute of America to provide resources and information and to see where it goes. So everything with the book was kind of born out of that.

**Aimee:** That's amazing. And so yesterday you got a relaunch of this book. Can you give us a quickie? Tell me about what's new, tell me about the book.

**Pam:** Yes. So Victor E. Frankl and the Search for Meaning: 21 Reflections on Living Life with Purpose. Beacon Press, the director of the publishing house was in Vienna and the Frankl family gave her a copy of the book that we had called at the time Inspiring Wisdom of Victor Frankl and she loved it and she said, "Can we buy this and republish it as something that is a gift edition journal that would be something people could treasure?" And I mean, it's beautiful. It's got watermarked pages and it's very similar to the style of The Manage Her where you have a chapter and then you have a prompt and you do a little work with it. So, it's kind of a little bit of a journal that goes along with it. You can do your own work while you're reading the book. You work through the book.

**Aimee:** Exactly. And I think that the whole point - and we called that a reflection book and I noticed yours had similar questions - Victor Frankl wasn't so much about staying inside yourself analyzing everything you're doing. It was more about contemplation, reflection on your life but the most important part is to act on it. So we can think all we want about what's going on. We can get angry at different things. We can think of all the things we would have, should have, could have done. But really what we need to do is to act on our intuitive sense of where we need to go and what we need to do because we're being guided by our conscience and we need to trust it. But the most important thing we need to do is act.

So the prompts are meant to - as yours are - to get the reader to reflect about how the principles apply to their own lives and then figure out ways that they might try something, go out in the world and engage with it in a different way.

**Pam:** Absolutely. I always say maybe start with something small - volunteer a little bit or if there's something that has meaning to you whether it's a soup kitchen or whatever you find meaning, wherever you see that the world needs you, volunteer there. Do you love libraries? Where do you see yourself? Where could you just add a little bit of meaning in your life by volunteering?

**Aimee:** Well, and he defined it as what is meant. He viewed it as objective and our free will to respond was subjective. So the meaning is calling to us. It's our responsibility to pull it into reality. And we find it three ways. We find it through the work we do, the creative works we do - writing a book, films, whatever. The deeds we do. We get it through experiences - nature, beauty, loving another human being. Or the last one - when we can't control the situation and let's say a diagnosis or somebody dies - our attitude that we take. We have the power to express ourselves in the world in a way that aligns with the values of what we want to show and what we want to model.

So we have a lot of opportunity to do that and we're called to if we listen. Again I think we're so used to listening to what other people tell us we should do or what society says. But when I work with young people, I mentor a lot of young people, I'll say so what do you do when you lose track of time? What is it that really gets you in the flow and then maybe that can't be their job. I'm not somebody who says oh okay you like that, you work - I mean, maybe you have to work a job like my middle daughter does. She works for a corporation and it's not her favorite thing. I mean, it's fun. She enjoys it enough, but she loves writing screenplays. She loves doing improv. I'm like, okay, then that's a means to an end.

So, your job could be a means to an end, a calling or a profession. But in today's world as you know with all the tech changing how we are living and working and playing and learning, that world is in such upheaval right now that career is a misnomer. What that job might be is a misnomer. But there's such an opportunity for creation. We can do things we couldn't do 10 years ago. You can start a podcast, you can write a book, you can do so many things. So it just starts - as Rumi said - when you start walking the way, the way appears. But you have to start walking the way. So your suggestion to start going out volunteering is very -

**Pam:** Yeah. Just dip your toe in it because it really is - it's just getting into a place of action even if it's a small amount, a safe way of being in action. Just do something and see if it might just call to you a little bit more or if an opportunity arises from that experience. You meet a person - that's how my life has been.

**Aimee:** Mine too. And you have to be a little uncomfortable. I think one of the things with my Be Unbound initiative that I do is that I work with educators to develop this and it's really about getting uncomfortable because discomfort is the price of a meaningful life. Susan David said that. But that uncomfortableness has to come from doing these little steps out into the world. Otherwise, we end up with a fragility where they don't want to try anything new because they don't have that muscle of what it means to be uncomfortable. And it's really important that we go ahead and take that first step even though we won't know anybody. And I know you have this too with your children where they'll say, "But I don't know anybody." "It's okay. Just take the first day. You will." And developing that strength of character to be able to step into the world as yourself as best you can and allow the discomfort. Allow the discomfort.

**Pam:** Totally. Well, it's the only way that you become a resilient person is by - it's a muscle that you have to strengthen. And you do it by doing things that are outside your comfort zone. Do a little something a little uncomfortable every day if you can and you'll just get very resilient, and you'll learn a lot.

**Aimee:** Well, that was my concern with how we're raising kids in this very narrow trajectory in school where they live a theoretical life, but they don't have - and it's all adult-driven. It's all guided with bumper rails and there is not that opportunity for them to go out and explore and be in the community and have different intergenerational relationships that can help them grow. I think we have done a disservice to humanity by not allowing all these unique individuals to really understand that they are unique and that they have something to contribute and it's their job to go find it. And instead we're saying, well, we got to fit you to this economy, which by the way, that economy is crumbling and the new one's emerging and we don't know -

**Pam:** It's changing. It's very much in flux. We don't know what the new - we don't know what it's going to look like in the fourth industrial revolution as things are changing with technology and AI.

**Aimee:** We're in it. We're in it. It's not even coming. It's we're in it. And so when we think of feeding our children through these mechanistic systems thinking they'll come out the other end and they'll have the job and be happy - it's not even human life. It doesn't allow us to feel and express and explore and learn how humans learn which is through relationships and engagement and exploration and discomfort and all of that.

So my work is in building the bridges away from those in parallel because they're pretty dominant part of our lives but to create that mycelium in our community that we all energetically support each other - which you did when you did the little micro school pod and stepped in. That is what we need to do and not everybody can do that but if you have somebody who's doing it, how can you support? How do you encourage?

I think that's the power of women historically was that we pulled together and we did things together. We washed the clothes together. We took care of the kids, whatever we were doing. We have different opportunities now. But that strength comes from not trying to do everything by ourselves, adhering to somebody else's demand of what a successful family is and how we have to look out in the community to really be accepted - not to belong because belonging is showing up as ourselves but fit in. And I think we're all getting pretty tired of that right now.

**Pam:** I think so too. Yeah. And I think that there's such a disconnect for people. I think that social media has created sort of this artificial sense of belonging in society. But I think now more than ever people are very lonely and I think the loneliness is contributing to the mental health problem in the US. We've never had more people. There's so many people and yet you're so alone. And I think it's that our mechanistic society has kept us so busy like cogs in a wheel chasing our tails that we're not building these relationships and creating these - we need to create more places for us to actually be together in the world. Creating more traditions where we're actually together with our friends, our families, our neighbors. Those kinds of things are really what make humans happy, being these social animals. It's being the real social, not social media.

**Aimee:** Yes. Well, I think Victor Frankl - and this has been going on prior to social media, it was just kryptonite because we were so isolated in our worlds that it had power that it probably normally wouldn't have if we truly were engaged pursuing meaningful pursuits and relationships. Victor Frankl called it the existential vacuum. So, what happens when you can't express, search for meaning? You are limited in what you can do. You can always still find meaning but it creates this vacuum that gets - and you say aggression - it creates or can create aggression, depression and addiction. So the existential vacuum is our world today.

And by the way, it happens in welfare societies and it happens in affluent capitalistic societies because we are not made to be at equilibrium. Meaning is about tension. There's always something to do. It creates a tension that moves us from who we are to what we can become. So the minute we try and think, oh, okay, I'm there. There's nothing going on. We've lost our purpose. We're here to constantly grow and learn and engage and there is never a time for us not to do that. So when we try to give it through material means by either welfare state or capitalistic, it doesn't work. It doesn't work.

**Pam:** Yeah. And I think that as we move forward there needs to be a level of intention that's set. And I do believe - I would love to see men be in a leadership position on this. But I really do feel that women have become very much leaders in our society. And I do think that women need to set the intention of creating intentional relationships, communities, traditions where we get ourselves, our spouses and our children involved in real communities and real activities in real life. Because if we don't do that I already see how technology is consuming this next generation. It's stealing their time, their motivation, their creativity. And not just in the children - I'm seeing it also in the young adults and even in the young parents where there's such a feeling or a sense that you're connected to community because you're connected on social media. 

So instead of getting together with people and friends in real life and having a barbecue with your whole neighborhood, you're getting on and you're doom scrolling for two hours.

**Aimee:** I think everybody is. And this goes back to the only control we have over it - the only control we have is over ourselves is challenging ourselves not to get lost in that rabbit hole, too. And I think that's where your point to women and community and nurturing each other - I have done many groups of women where we bring them together to support each other in their inconvenient dreams. What did we give up that we didn't do because we were responding to all these things we were supposed to do? What part of ourselves didn't come along with that?

And in our society that we've had for a long time that just was no, you've got to go and break the board table. And I'm thinking, "No, I don't want to sit at a board table. I want to create something new and different." And that's our opportunity. And a friend of mine, Gretchen Leonard, is coming out with a book in October called Your Second Prime: Does Aging Suck or Do We Suck at Aging? 

And so she and I have led these groups of these women over 40 and it's been powerful to listen to how many women just really wow - either couldn't think of something for a moment because they're thinking I've never thought about what I would like to do that I haven't had a chance to do - and others who right away could name something that they're interested in doing. And so I think there's lots that we can do together as women to inspire the change in each other and support each other in doing that.

And I agree with you, it's really this shift towards something that is different from the mechanistic patriarchal only financial gain is how you're measured society. And that doesn't mean that men can't learn. It doesn't mean that men - they've been trained the way women have been trained and now it's not serving us and it's not serving our tribe.

**Pam:** I think they miss community as much as we do. I think that men also need their tribe, and I know women do because we're so verbal and we really we do. I'm so happy I have my little office hours group and we get together every other Friday and we just honestly - Brendan's asking, "What do you guys do?" I'm thinking, "We talk non-stop for like four hours."

**Aimee:** Yes. And we need it. I've always had that. I mean, even when I was the weird one out, people were still interested. What are you doing? And to have people to bounce ideas off and just find out how left field I really was. And now I'm not. When I put my kids in alternative education, there was only three types of schools that were a little bit different from your traditional school. And now there's over 50 different models of ways that we actually have the power to create a different kind of life if we choose to not follow the same trajectories we've been promised would lead to a successful life.

So if you could just really quickly - I know you've been involved with Be Unbound - could you tell my moms about this model? It's very interesting.

**Pam:** Yes. So it started when I was doing these films highlighting the systems - the Inside the Machine that we were feeding our kids to - whether it was foster care and we did three foster care, the overprescribing of meds to kids and education that there was only one way out. They had to compete with each other and get to a really good school.
